coredata.dc:title: White-light-emitting hydrogels with self-healing properties and adjustable emission colors
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Yangdaiyi
Ding, Zeyang
Liu, Yu
Zhang, Yuping
Jiang, Shimei
coredata.dc:description: White-light-emitting soft materials with self-healing properties show extensive applications in many fields. Herein, a novel self-healing hydrogel is successfully fabricated using oxidized dextran (Odex) and dithiodipropionate dihydrazide (TPH). Carbon dots (CDs), Riboflavin (Ri) and Rhodamine B (RhB) are incorporated into the gel matrix to produce white light emission through fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) process, thus achieving excellent Commission Internationale de L’eclairage (CIE) coordinate value of (0.30, 0.33). The emission colors can be easily tuned via changing proportions of three emitters or the excitation wavelength. When the hydrogels are coated on an ultraviolet light-emitting diodes (UV LED), the hydrogel coating converts UV light to white light and repairs itself in 20 h while a hole is dug from it. Thanks to reversible exchanging reactions of acylhydrazone and disulfide bonds in hydrogel networks, the hydrogel coating exhibits perfect self-healing property in a wide range of pH (from 5 to 9 except for 7). The excellent emission and self-healing properties of hydrogels have great value in practical applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: White-light-emitting
Hydrogels
Self-healing
Fluorescence resonance energy-transfer
Dynamic covalent bonds

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of Odex/TPH hydrogels and white emitting hydrogels
Rheological analyses and mechanical property tests
Dual responsive sol–gel transitions of the hydrogels
Preparation of white-light-emitting self-healing LED coatings
Characterization of Odex, TPH and hydrogels
Sol-gel transitions of hydrogels upon dual stimuli

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Reversible sol–gel transitions under (a) acid/base and (b) redox stimuli of Gel-1.

UV–vis absorption and fluorescence spectra of CDs (blue, λex = 340 nm, λem = 445 nm), Ri (green, λex = 373/445 nm, λem = 533 nm), and RhB (red, λex = 553 nm, λem = 577 nm) in aqueous solution. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

Fluorescence spectra of (a) CDs aqueous solution (0.0075 mg mL−1) on gradual addition of Ri (λex = 365 nm) and (c) Ri aqueous solution (0.0075 mg mL−1) on gradual addition of RhB (λex = 445 nm). (b) Intensity ratio (I0/I) vs Ri concentration for emission peak of CDs. (d) Intensity ratio (I0/I) vs RhB concentration for emission peak of Ri. (e) Fluorescence spectra of the white-light-emitting solution and hydrogel with the same composition of CDs (0.012 mg mL−1), Ri (0.0075 mg mL−1), RhB (1.92*10-3 mg mL−1) (λex = 365 nm). (f) Corresponding CIE coordinates for white-light-emitting solution (0.30, 0.34) and white-light-emitting gel (0.30, 0.33).
(a) Time, (b) frequency and (c) strain amplitude sweep of G' and G'' for WL-Gel. (d) Recovery test for WL-Gel, alternating strain amplitudes of 10% and 600%.

(a) Illustrations of the fabrication for white-light-emitting self-healing hydrogel coatings. (b) Photographs of UV LED (left) and UV LED coated with white-light-emitting hydrogel (right). (c) CIE coordinates of coatings with different proportions of emitters. (d) Fluorescence spectra and (e) CIE coordinates of WL-Gel with different excitation wavelength (from 335 nm to 415 nm).
Self-healing properties of the WL-Gel based down-converting coatings at pH of (a) 5, (b) 7 and (c) 9. (d) Illustrations of self-healing process at different pH.
Fabrication process of white-light-emitting self-healing hydrogels and the applications on UV LED down-converting coatings.
Hydrogels composed of different oxidation degrees and molar ratios at pH of 6.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Molar ratio was calculated by n(–NHNH2)/n(–CHO).

head.ce:title: White-light-emitting hydrogels with self-healing properties and adjustable emission colors

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Engineering Research Center of Organic and Polymer Optoelectronic Materials

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Supramolecular Structure and Materials

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Supramolecular Structure and Materials, College of Chemistry, Jilin University, Changchun 130012, PR China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Engineering Research Center of Organic and Polymer Optoelectronic Materials, Ministry of Education, College of Chemistry, Jilin University, Changchun 130012, PR China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Engineering Research Center of Organic and Polymer Optoelectronic Materials

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: White-light-emitting self-healing hydrogels were prepared. The hydrogels can heal the damaged part under a wide range of pH and the emission colors are easily adjustable.

White-light-emitting soft materials with self-healing properties show extensive applications in many fields. Herein, a novel self-healing hydrogel is successfully fabricated using oxidized dextran (Odex) and dithiodipropionate dihydrazide (TPH). Carbon dots (CDs), Riboflavin (Ri) and Rhodamine B (RhB) are incorporated into the gel matrix to produce white light emission through fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) process, thus achieving excellent Commission Internationale de L’eclairage (CIE) coordinate value of (0.30, 0.33). The emission colors can be easily tuned via changing proportions of three emitters or the excitation wavelength. When the hydrogels are coated on an ultraviolet light-emitting diodes (UV LED), the hydrogel coating converts UV light to white light and repairs itself in 20 h while a hole is dug from it. Thanks to reversible exchanging reactions of acylhydrazone and disulfide bonds in hydrogel networks, the hydrogel coating exhibits perfect self-healing property in a wide range of pH (from 5 to 9 except for 7). The excellent emission and self-healing properties of hydrogels have great value in practical applications.

ce:section.ce:para: White-light-emitting soft materials have gained tremendous attractions due to their potential applications in light sources and sensors [1,2].To construct such materials, a common way is integrating emitters with three (red, green and blue) or two (yellow and blue) different primary colors into gel matrices through fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) process [1,3,4]. In this way, a wide variety of emitters, such as organic dyes [5,6], quantum dots [7–9], carbon dots [10,11], rare earth metals [12,13] and inorganic–organic hybrids [14], can be chosen. However, most of them suffer from some drawbacks such as toxicity, tedious organic syntheses, aggregation-caused quenching (ACQ) and mining problems, which increase the difficulty for the fabrication of white-light-emitting soft materials. It remains a problem for scientists to select emitters with hypotoxicity, low prices and matching emission properties. For another, hydrogels made of biomaterials, as a novel gel matrix, not only provide a stable environment to encapsulate various emitters but also inhibit the aggregation behaviors of water-soluble ACQ emitters to ensure efficient emission [15]. Nowadays, on account of the inherent green, sustainable and malleable nature of hydrogels [16], the white-light-emitting soft materials based on hydrogels are applied to solid-state lighting [17–19], message storing [6] and stimuli-responsive materials aspects [4,20]. However, it can’t be ignored that hydrogels are easily damaged. It is still a challenge to build a sustainable white-light-emitting soft material.

Self-healing hydrogels are expected to be good candidates. In comparison to common hydrogels, self-healing hydrogels possess extra ability to repair themselves when getting damaged. Self-healing hydrogels are fabricated by incorporating dynamic covalent or non-covalent bonds into polymer networks. The dynamic nature endows materials with wide application prospects in fields of drug delivery [21,22], 3D cell culture [22,23], wound healing [24], shape memory [25,26] and wearable devices [27]. Especially, the dynamic covalent bonds like disulfide bonds [23,28], Diel-Alder reactions [29–31], borate ester [26,32] and schiff bases [21,33,34] possess the reversibility of non-covalent bonds as well as the stability of covalent bonds, increasing the stability of hydrogels. However, these self-healing hydrogels based on single dynamic covalent bonds can only repair themselves in particular conditions like acid, base, heating, which inhibits the application range of self-healing hydrogels.

With these considerations in mind, a new type of self-healing hydrogel was synthesized using oxidized dextran (Odex) and dithiodipropionate dihydrazide (TPH) (Scheme 1 ). The disulfide bonds and newly generated acylhydrazone bonds could reversibly break and bond under acid/base conditions [23,35], which allowed the hydrogels to heal the damage under a wide range of pH. In order to endow the hydrogels with fluorescence properties, three water-soluble and environmental-friendly emitters were selected and doped into above self-healing hydrogels: carbon dots (CDs), Riboflavin (Ri) and Rhodamine B (RhB). Owing to the matched absorption and emission peaks of three emitters, white light emission could be achieved through fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) process, leading to a white-light-emitting soft material with an outstanding Commission Internationale de L’ eclairage (CIE) coordinate value of (0.30, 0.33). Interestingly, the emission colors were easily tunable with the change of proportions of three emitters or excitation wavelength for the unique excitation-dependent photoluminescence behavior of CDs [36]. The fluorescent hydrogels were further coated on an ultraviolet light-emitting diodes (UV LED) to convert UV light to white light. The hydrogel coating can repair itself in less than 20 h when a hole is dug from it under a wide range of pH. The self-healing abilities and adjustable emission colors of hydrogels show enormous potential in developing novel optical devices.

All reagents and solvents were obtained from commercial supplies and used directly without further purification unless otherwise stated. The 1H NMR spectra were recorded on a Bruker Avance III 500 MHz NMR spectrometer. UV–Vis absorption spectra were recorded on a Shimadzu 3600 UV–Vis-near-IR spectrophotometer. Fluorescence spectroscopic studies were performed on a Shimadzu RF-5301PC spectrofluorometer. Scanning electron microscope (SEM) images were obtained from a JEOL JEM-6700F scanning electron microscope with 3 kV operating voltage. Fourier-transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were recorded on a Bruker VERTEX 70 FTIR spectrometer. Rheological measurements were carried out on a TA HR-2 rheometer with parallel plate geometry. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurements were performed on a Thermo ESCALAB 250 X-ray photoelectron spectrometer. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was conducted on a JEOL JEM-2100F under an accelerating voltage of 200 kV. The mechanical property tests were carried out using a universal testing machine (ITW 5944 2KN) at a compressing speed of 0.5 mm/min at room temperature.

Odex was synthetized according to previous reports [37–39]. Briefly, dextran (8 g, 50 mmol, MW 70000) and oxidant NaIO4 (1.06 g, 5 mmol) were dissolved in 60 mL deionized water. The mixture was stirred in dark at room temperature for 24 h to obtain transparent pale-yellow solution. Diethylene glycol (4 mL) was added to the solution to remove unreacted NaIO4. Then, the solution was dialyzed exhaustively (MWCO 8000–14000) for 3 days against water and lyophilized for 2 days to get white floc (Odex).

Generally, the oxidation degree, which is defined as the molar ratio of oxidized glucose units to initial dextran monomer ones [39], of Odex depends on the amount of NaIO4. In our system, because only 0.1 equiv. NaIO4, versus dextran, was used, the theoretical oxidation degree was 10%. To measure the actual oxidation degree of Odex, a conventional aldimine condensation reaction between aldehyde and tert-butyl carbazate was utilized [38,39]. Owing to the ultrahigh yield (close to 100%) of the reaction, the amount of aldehyde as well as oxidation degree can be easily measured through the quantitative analysis of 1H NMR spectra. The detailed experimental procedure are as follows. Odex (0.5 g, 3 mmol) and tert-butyl carbazate (2 g, 15 mmol) were dissolved in 15 mL acetate buffer (pH = 5.5). The mixture was stirred at room temperature for 24 h. Afterwards, NaBH3CN (0.94 g, 15 mmol) was added to the mixture to remove excess tert-butyl carbazate. After another 12 h, acetone was added to the mixture and the product precipitated out. Then, the mixture was filtered to remove liquid to give white precipitate. The product was further freeze-dried for 1H NMR measurements. Through the integral results of peaks at 1.4 ppm (tert-butyl, reflecting oxidized units) and that at 4.9 ppm (anomeric proton of dextran, reflecting all the dextran units) (Fig. S1), the oxidation degree was calculated to be 7%.
In this way, when 0.2 equiv. NaIO4 was used, Odex product with 14% oxidation degree was obtained.

TPH was also synthetized according to reported work [40]. 3,3′-Dithiopropionic acid dimethylester (975.4 μl, 4.2 mmol) and hydrazine hydrate (1.24 mL, 25 mmol) were dissolved in 25 mL MeOH. The mixture was stirred at room temperature for 12 h to obtain white suspensions. Then, the suspensions were filtered to obtain white filter residue, which was further washed with MeOH for 3 times. 0.92 g white powder was reached, yield: 75%.

On the basis of the report [36], 1 g citric acid and 335 µl ethylenediamine were dissolved in 10 mL deionized water. The mixture was put into a poly (tetrafluoroethylene) (Teflon)-lined autoclave (30 mL) and heated at 200 °C for 5 h. The cooled mixture was then dialyzed (MWCO 3500) against water for 2 days. The solution outside the dialysis bag was collected and freeze-dried to obtain brown-black powder (CDs). FT-IR: CO stretching vibrations (1652 cm−1), NH bending vibrations (1546 cm−1), C-NH-C asymmetric stretching vibrations (1145 cm−1) (Fig. S2a). XPS results revealed the existence of C, N, O elements. Meanwhile, three different types of carbon atoms (graphitic or aliphatic (CC and CC), oxygenated, and nitrous) were discovered by C1s analysis (Fig. S2b-c). Moreover, the morphologies of CDs were fully characterized by TEM, which showed dispersive particles with uniform diameter of 2–6 nm (Fig. S2d).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of Odex/TPH hydrogels and white emitting hydrogels
ce:section.ce:para: Odex/TPH hydrogels could be easily prepared by mixing Odex and TPH aqueous solution at room temperature. Detailed gelation procedures were as follows. 87.5 mg Odex (–CHO = 0.038 mmol) and 2.25 mg TPH (–NHNH2 = 0.019 mmol) were dissolved in 0.35 mL and 0.15 mL 0.25 M acetate buffer (pH = 6), respectively. The two solutions were mixed to obtain stable hydrogel in less than one minute. The molar ratio (–NHNH2: –CHO) and oxidation degree were 0.5 and 7%, respectively. Other gels with different oxidation degrees and molar ratios were prepared with the same procedure as Gel-1 (Table 1 ).
Gel-2: 87.5 mg Odex (oxidation degree = 7%, –CHO = 0.038 mmol) and 4.5 mg TPH (–NHNH2 = 0.038 mmol) were needed. The molar ratio was 1.
Gel-3: 87.5 mg Odex (oxidation degree = 7%, –CHO = 0.038 mmol) and 9 mg TPH (–NHNH2 = 0.076 mmol) were required. The molar ratio was 2.
Gel-4: 87.5 mg Odex (oxidation degree = 14%, –CHO = 0.076 mmol) and 4.5 mg TPH (–NHNH2 = 0.038 mmol) were needed. The molar ratio was 0.5.

White-light-emitting hydrogels (WL-Gel) were fabricated as follows. Firstly, white-light-emitting solution was prepared by mixing CDs (0.012 mg mL−1), Ri (0.0075 mg mL−1) and RhB (1.92*10-3 mg mL−1) in 0.25 M acetate buffer (pH = 6). Secondly, 87.5 mg Odex (–CHO = 0.038 mmol) and 2.25 mg TPH (–NHNH2 = 0.019 mmol) was dissolved in 0.35 mL and 0.15 mL aforementioned white-light-emitting solution, respectively. Finally, by mixing the two solutions, we obtained WL-Gel in less than one minute. The molar ratio and oxidation degree were 0.5 and 7%, which was the same as that of Gel-1. All the hydrogels were stabilized for 24 h before testing.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Rheological analyses and mechanical property tests
ce:section.ce:para: The rheological measurements were performed with a flat plate of 20 mm diameter. The time sweep experiment was carried out with constant angular frequency (10 rad/s) and strain (1.0%) to evaluate the strength of hydrogels. To test the gelation time, two precursor solutions were mixed homogeneously and transferred onto a 40 mm parallel plate to monitor G’ and G” with the change of time at room temperature. The frequency sweep experiment was conducted under a fixed strain of 1.0% with the variation of angular frequency from 0.1 to 100 rad/s. Gel-1 was prepared in a specific vessel to form a specific cylinder shape with 239.98 mm2 base area for mechanical measurements using a universal testing machine. Then the compression test was carried out at a compressing speed of 0.5 mm/min at room temperature.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Dual responsive sol–gel transitions of the hydrogels
ce:section.ce:para: Gel-1 transformed into a transparent solution with the addition of HCl solution (30 μl, 6 M) when being heated at 60 ℃ for 30 min. Then, upon 25 μl triethylamine (TEA), the sol came to gel again. As to redox stimuli behaviors, Gel-1 collapsed with the addition of 4 mg 1,4-dithio- dl -threitol (DTT). The gel was formed again when being treated with 5 μl H2O2. The reversible sol–gel transitions upon DTT/ H2O2 happened at room temperature.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of white-light-emitting self-healing LED coatings
ce:section.ce:para: GaN LEDs were bought from Advanced Optoeletronic Technology Co., Ltd. The peak wavelength is 369 nm and the half-peak breadth is 13 nm. The radiation flux is 129.039 mW and the power of the LED is 1.2291 W under the voltage of 3.521 V and current of 0.34998 A.

WL-Gel was used to fabricate hydrogel coatings. Briefly, 87.5 mg Odex (–CHO = 0.038 mmol) and 2.25 mg TPH (–NHNH2 = 0.019 mmol) was dissolved in 0.35 mL and 0.15 mL aforementioned white-light-emitting solution (as shown in Section 2.5), respectively. The two solutions were dropped into a glass hemisphere with an inner diameter of 15 mm. The glass was further rotated to make homogeneous hydrogels cover the whole inner surface of the glass. The hydrogel coating was easily prepared in one minute. Then this coating was placed on the GaN LED to convert UV light to white light.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of Odex, TPH and hydrogels
ce:section.ce:para: Odex and TPH were prepared through an oxidation reaction of dextran and an aminolysis of ester reaction, respectively. Experimental details and associated characterization data are given in experimental section and supporting information (Fig. S3-S4). Among them, the newly generated aldehyde groups of Odex could be confirmed by FT-IR and 1H NMR measurements. In Fig. S5, the spectra of Odex showed an extra band at 1730 cm−1, referring to the stretching vibrations of carbonyl (CO), in comparison to that of dextran [37]. As to 1H NMR spectra, some additional peaks of Odex appeared in the range of 4.2–5.8 ppm, which were assigned as the proton peaks of hemiacetal structures from the reaction of aldehyde groups and original hydroxyl ones [38], suggesting dextran were oxidized successfully to produce Odex (Fig. S3). The oxidation degree of Odex was tuned to be 7% and 14% by controlling the content of oxidant and dextran. Corresponding measuring methods were shown in Section 2.2.

Odex and TPH couldn’t form hydrogels in water individually. Fortunately, mixture of Odex with TPH in acetate buffer (pH = 6) could lead to obvious gelation behaviors at room temperature. The disulfide bonds and newly generated acylhydrazone bonds are able to serve as linkages to construct three-dimensional (3D) hydrogel networks. The gelation tests were systematacially investigated further. Gel-1, Gel-2 and Gel-3 were obtained upon varying molar ratios of –NHNH2: –CHO (from 0.5 to 2) with constant oxidation degree (7%). Gel-1 and Gel-4 were achieved when altering oxidation degree (from 7% to 14%) with constant molar ratio (0.5) (Table 1). Detailed experimental process and data were shown in Section 2.5. It is noted that the gels became opaque with the increase of TPH on account of the increase of hydrophobic part (disulfide bonds) in gel system. Only Gel-1 was transparent, which was in favor of further development in visual display aspects.

Further studies were aimed at a better elucidation of the driving force responsible for gelation. In the FT-IR spectra of freeze-dried Gel-1, two absorption peaks appeared at 1575 cm−1 and 1675 cm−1, which were indexed to amide bending vibration (NH) and imine stretching vibration (CN), respectively, verifying the formation of acylhydrazone linkages between Odex and TPH (Fig. S5) [23,38]. Thus, polymer chains (Odex) were cross-linked to form 3D networks via the linkages (acylhydrazone) reacted by aldehyde and acylhydrazide. The networks further immobilized water to lead to hydrogel formation. In addition, the microstructure of freeze-dried Gel-1 was shown by SEM, which expressed 3D hydrogel networks with irregular porous structures (Fig. S6). Such irregular porous structures might be caused by the aggregation of hydrophobic disulfide bonds [35].

Rheological measurements provide not only accurate standard of gelation judgment but also mechanical property characterization. All the gels exhibited a higher elastic storage modulus (G’) and a lower loss modulus (G”), indicating a solid-like hydrogel state [35]. More importantly, with the increase of molar ratios of –NHNH2: –CHO (from 0.5 to 2), that is, from Gel-1 to Gel-2 to Gel-3, the G’ increased gradually, implying increased strength of gels (Fig. S7a). Similarly, when the oxidation degree was changed from 7% (Gel-1) to 14% (Gel-4) with a constant molar ratio (0.5), the G’ also exhibited obvious enhancement (Fig. S7b). All these phenomena manifested that more production of acylhydrazone bonds could contribute to a better mechanical strength of hydrogel.

More abundant mechanical properties of hydrogels were probed using Gel-1. The gelation time was evaluated firstly by dropping solutions of Odex and TPH to the 40 mm parallel plate to monitor the time-dependent G’ and G”. After only 40 s at room temperature, the G’ surpassed G”, suggesting the gel formation (Fig. S8a) [23]. And Gel-1 was stable with the increase of frequency until 100 rad/s (Fig. S8b). Then, elastic response of Gel-1 was tested through strain amplitude sweep, and the G’ decreased rapidly and dropped below G” when the oscillation strain was above 450%, indicating the collapse of gel structure (Fig. S8c). Recovery experiments of Gel-1 were operated with a low strain (10%), where G’ surpassed G”, and a high strain (600%), where G’ was lower than G”, alternately, revealing the quick recovery properties of the polymer network and outstanding self-healing abilities of the dynamic covalent bonds-based hydrogels (Fig. S8d). Additionally, compressing tests were performed to investigate ultimate mechanical properties of Gel-1 using a universal testing machine (Fig. S9). The stress–strain curves displayed a fracture strain above 72%, a fracture stress of 21644 Pa (≈ 5.2 N, area of circle hydrogels = 239.98 mm2).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sol-gel transitions of hydrogels upon dual stimuli
ce:section.ce:para: On account of the dynamic acylhydrazone and disulfide bonds, which are sensitive to acid/base and redox, outstanding sol–gel transitions were expected [41]. With the addition of HCl at 60 ℃ for 30 min, Gel-1 collapsed and transformed into solution owing to the breakage of acylhydrazone bonds under severe acid conditions (Fig. 1 a). The acylhydrazone bonds would recover when pH was above 4. Thus, the solution turned back to the gel state again upon TEA stimulus. As to redox response, similar gel-to-sol transition was observed upon reductant DTT at room temperature due to the breakage of disulfide bonds. Also, the addition of oxidant H2O2 could lead to the reformation of gel (Fig. 1b). All the reversible gel-sol transformations could be repeated for more than 3 times.

The fabrication of a white-light-emitting soft material needs not only a stable gel matrix (Gel-1), but also three (red, green and blue) or two (yellow and blue) emitters with different primary colors. Here, nontoxic CDs, Ri and RhB were selected on account of their highly efficient emissions in water. At first, the optical properties of three emitters were studied in aqueous solution. Fully matched (overlapped) absorption and emission peaks of three emitters indicated that CDs/Ri and Ri/RhB could be served as ideal donor/acceptor pairs for FRET process and further construction of white-light-emitting system (Fig. 2 ) [42,43].

The FRET processes from CDs to Ri, Ri to RhB were elucidated, respectively. As shown in Fig. 3 a, there was a gradual decrease of emission intensity of CDs on gradual addition of Ri to CDs aqueous solution, revealing obvious energy transfer from CDs (donor) to Ri (acceptor). Similar decreased emission intensity of Ri was also observed with the addition of RhB to Ri aqueous solution, revealing energy transfer from Ri (donor) to RhB (acceptor) (Fig. 3c). To figure out whether all the emission quenching of donor was attributed to the FRET processes, Stern-Volmer plot, which reveals a functional relation between I0 /I and acceptor concentration, was applied. It can be explained from three equations as follows.
(1)
I 0
/
I
= 1 +
k a
q
(2)
I 0
/
I
= 1 +
τ 0
k q
q
(3)
I 0
/
I
=
1 +
k a
q
1 +
τ 0
k q
q
where I0 and I are the emission intensities of donor at the absence and presence of quencher (acceptor), [q] is the concentration of quencher (acceptor), k a is the association constant of complex, k q is the bimolecular quenching constant, and τ0 is the lifetime of donor, respectively [3]. Equation (1) represents dynamic quenching which occurs due to the FRET from donor to acceptor at excited state. Equation (2) represents the static quenching which means the formation of non-luminescent complex at ground state. If both dynamic and static quenching exist in the system, equation (3) is followed.

Thus, for CDs/Ri, a bending upward curve was observed, which obeyed equation (3), demonstrating the existence of both static quenching and dynamic quenching (Fig. 3b). The dynamic quenching could be comprehended for the good overlap of CDs and Ri. The static quenching was further confirmed by UV–vis spectra (Fig. S10). Gradually red-shifted absorption bands of CDs (341 nm to 350 nm) with the addition of Ri were noticed, suggesting the existence of interactions between CDs and Ri as well as the formation of complex, which caused static quenching [3]. As far as Ri/RhB was concerned, a straight line was observed, which obeys equation (2), that is, dynamic quenching was followed (Fig. 3d). However, other than FRET, inner filter effects can also lead to emission quenching of donor. To make it clear, fluorescence lifetime measurements were employed. Fluorescence lifetime parameter is independent of inner filter effects and concentrations of fluorescent agents, but has to do with non-radiative energy transfer (FRET) [44]. In Fig. S11, the average lifetimes decreased from 4.87 ns (Ri) to 4.23 ns (Ri/RhB), indicating definite FRET process from Ri to RhB. What’s more, no obvious FRET process was discovered when RhB was added to the solution of CDs because of the poor overlap between the absorption and emission peaks of RhB and CDs (Fig. 2, S12). Above all, utilizing the continuous FRET from CDs to Ri, Ri to RhB, we achieved an excellent white-light-emitting system in aqueous solution with constant composition of CDs (0.012 mg mL−1), Ri (0.0075 mg mL−1) and RhB (1.92*10-3 mg mL−1). The CIE value was measured to be (0.30, 0.34) (Fig. 3e, f).

In comparison to solution state, white-light-emitting solid-state materials show more significant applications. Therefore, the three emitters (CDs, Ri, RhB) were incorporated into the aforementioned transparent Gel-1 to fabricate white-light-emitting soft materials. Though CDs, Ri and RhB are non-luminescent in aggregation state owing to the ACQ effect, the semi-solid hydrogel can make the emitters disperse evenly in the system, guaranteeing both highly efficient emissions and fully FRET behaviors of emitters. As expected, the emission spectrum of the WL-Gel showed broad emission peaks covering the visible area (Fig. 3e). The energy transfer efficiency was 62.52% and 18.74% from CDs to Ri, Ri to RhB, respectively (Fig. S13). In this way, a white-light-emitting hydrogel (WL-Gel) with a great CIE value (0.30, 0.33) was achieved (Fig. 3f). The mechanical properties of WL-Gel were also investigated, which were similar to that of Gel-1 (Fig. 4 ). The similarity indicated the introduction of emitters didn’t influence the own nature and structure of gel.

To further explore display applications of self-healing white-light-emitting hydrogels, we coated WL-Gel on the UV LED. As prepared in the Section 2.8, the Odex and TPH white-light-emitting solutions were dropped into a glass hemisphere, which was rotated to form homogeneous hydrogels on the inner surface. Then the coating was placed on an LED to prepare white light LED (Fig. 5 a, b).

For commercial LEDs applications, LEDs with multiple emission colors are meaningful and hardly adjustable. In our system, the emission colors of coatings were modulated with the change of emitter proportions. As shown in Fig. 5c, a series of multi-color tunable LEDs were realized from blue to green to orange, covering a wide range of correlated color coordinates. In comparison to the change of emitter proportions, a more convenient method, varying the excitation wavelength, was adopted on account of the unique excitation-dependent photoluminescence behavior of CDs [36] (Fig. S14). With irradiation of different excitation light (from 335 nm to 415 nm), the emission peaks of WL-Gel exhibited great shifts and intense variation, which was attributed to the different emissions of CDs and the varied FRET efficiencies of hydrogels (Fig. 5d). Meanwhile, the corresponding CIE coordinates moved sharply from blue region to yellow region, leading to multi-color emissions (Fig. 5e). These simple and environmental-friendly methods of emission color modulation had potentials for greatly simplifying LED fabrications in the future.

Generally, soft material coatings are easily damaged. A new preparation needs more costs. To solve this problem, in our system, the introduction of acylhydrazone bonds and disulfides endows hydrogels with desirable self-healing properties. As expected, when a hole was dug from the center of WL-Gel, surrounding hydrogels gradually moved to the hole and it could be completely healed within 20 h (Fig. S15). Additionally, due to the fact that dynamic exchanges of acylhydrazone and disulfide need acid and base conditions, the self-healing abilities were further tested in WL-Gel coatings at different pH. Since the three emitters were stable from pH 5 to 10 (Fig. S16), the pH value was set as 5 and 9 by adjusting the acetate buffer (Fig. 6 ). A hole was dug from the WL-Gel coatings. After 20 h in sealed environment, both acidic and basic WL-Gel coatings healed the holes, indicating the excellent self-healing properties. It is noticed that no healing performance was realized at pH of 7, confirming that the dynamic exchanges of acylhydrazone and disulfide were locked under neutral conditions [35]. Under the composition of WL-Gel, the self-healing behaviors could be repeated for 3 times. For more cycles attempted, obvious imprint on holes could be observed. Furthermore, a contrast experiment was performed using white-light-emitting agar hydrogel coatings (0.4 wt%), which were known as a hydrogel without self-healing property (Fig. S17). The hole couldn’t be repaired even after 3 days, demonstrating self-healing coatings were scarce and meaningful laterally.

To evaluate the emission stability before and after healing, the fluorescence spectra of hydrogels in the healed part and surrounding undamaged region were measured (Fig. S18). The two samples exhibited nearly the same emission peaks and CIE results, revealing consistent proportions of three emitters. Correspondingly, the FRET efficiency of the well-dispersed emitters was maintained, leading to consistent emission properties after healing.

In summary, we successfully fabricated a self-healing hydrogel by mixing Odex solutions and TPH solutions. The disulfide bonds and newly generated acylhydrazone bonds linked the polymer chains to form 3D hydrogel networks. CDs (blue emission), Ri (green emission) and RhB (red emission), served as the emitters with well-matched emission and absorption spectra, were incorporated into the hydrogels. Upon irradiation, partial energy transferred from CDs to Ri and then excited RhB to gain mixed white light. In this way, a white-light-emitting hydrogel with an outstanding Commission Internationale de L’ eclairage (CIE) coordinate value of (0.30, 0.33) was achieved through FRET process. The versatile emission colors of the hydrogels could be also easily tuned with the change of proportions of three emitters or excitation wavelength for the unique excitation-dependent photoluminescence behavior of CDs. The hydrogels were further coated on UV LEDs to convert UV light to white light. More interestingly, when a hole was dug from the hydrogel coating, after 20 h, the hole repaired perfectly. The self-healing behaviors could occur in a wide range of pH (from 5 to 9 except 7) owing to the reversible exchanging reactions of dynamic colvalent acylhydrazone bonds and disulfide bonds under acid/basic conditions. In addition, the healed part and surrounding undamaged region expressed nearly the same emission features and CIE results, indicating outstanding emission stability before and after healing. The great self-healing properties and stable emission colors after healing process of the white-light-emitting hydrogels exhibited wonderful application potentials of optical devices in future.

Yangdaiyi Zhang: Conceptualization, Methodology, Software, Writing - original draft. Zeyang Ding: Investigation. Yu Liu: Methodology, Investigation. Yuping Zhang: Formal analysis, Investigation. Shimei Jiang: Conceptualization, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (51673082 and 21935005).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jcis.2020.08.080.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
